blurb: A Plant Select garden strain raised from Bruce Meyers' big-flowered penstemon crosses — sturdy spires of giant goblet flowers in a jewel-box mix of white, rose-pink, lavender and deep purple-violet over thick waxy gray-green leaves in early summer.
water: Low to moderate; xeric once established (overwatering shortens its life)
seasons: Giant white-to-purple goblet flowers late spring–early summer; thick waxy gray-green basal leaves; tan seed stalks into fall
wildlife: Bees and other pollinators; large flowers draw bumblebees
habitat: n/a — garden hybrid (wild parents of dry rocky plains & slopes)
gaps: Photos are species-representative of the parent species Penstemon grandiflorus (large lavender goblet flowers, waxy gray-green clasping leaves), honestly captioned: 'Prairie Jewel' is a garden strain with no clean iNaturalist taxon, and no open-licensed cultivar-specific photo set exists. The cultivar's flowers range across the same white/pink/lavender/purple jewel mix.

care.hardiness: Very cold-hardy across the Front Range — rated USDA zones 3–9, well within Boulder's 5b–6a, and good to about 6,200 ft elevation; like most large-flowered border penstemons it is naturally short-lived (often 2–4 years), so let it self-sow to persist.[1]
care.planting: Plant out in spring after the mid-May average last frost, or in early fall, into lean, fast-draining ground in the sunniest spot you have; do not amend with compost. In containers use a gritty, sharply draining mix and keep the crown dry — potted penstemon crowns rot far more readily than plants in open ground.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun — at least six hours — for upright, well-colored spikes; in shade it gets floppy and shy-flowering.[2]
care.soil: Wants sharp drainage and lean loamy or sandy soil; it resents heavy, wet clay and rich ground. Plant on a slope, in a rock garden, or in a gritty bed rather than amending the soil.[1][2]
care.water: Low to moderate, xeric once established; it needs good spring moisture to bloom, but overwatering — especially after flowering — brings on crown rot and is the main thing that shortens its already short life.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space about 12–15 in apart; clumps reach roughly 20–36 in tall in bloom and 8–12 in wide.[1]
care.propagation: Grow from seed, which germinates best after cold-moist stratification (fall-sow outdoors, or chill the moist seed in the fridge before a late-winter start); the strain comes fairly true from seed. Penstemons resent division — don't split the crown — so to clone a particular plant take basal or softwood cuttings instead.[2]
care.stratify: Cold-moist stratify the seed (fall sowing supplies the natural winter chilling) to break dormancy and germinate well.[2]
care.bloom: Giant goblet flowers in a jewel mix — pure white through lavender and rose-pink to deep purple-violet — in late spring and early summer; deadhead spent spikes to neaten the plant (and skip the first two years of heavy deadheading to let it set seed and self-sow for replacements).[1]
care.feeding: No fertilizer needed in normal soil — feeding makes floppy growth and shortens the plant's life.[2]
care.maintenance: Low: deadhead spent spikes, then back off summer water to prevent crown rot; tidy old flower stems in late fall or early spring, and leave some seed to drop so new seedlings replace the short-lived parents.[1][2]
care.troubles: No serious pests or diseases; root and crown rot strike in wet, poorly drained or over-amended soil, and rust, grasshoppers and flea beetles are occasional.[2]
care.companions: A bold front-of-border and rock-garden plant for hot, dry sites — combine with other xeric penstemons, salvias, grasses and lean-soil perennials.[1]

edible.summary: Grown ornamentally for its big jewel-toned flowers — no documented edible use.[2]
edible.caution: No known culinary use; not documented as poisonous, but it is not a food plant — don't eat it.[2]
